title: xenobots for space conquest
body:
one of the things i keep coming back to is: what happens to people whose edge was logic, once AI outspeeds them at logic?

i've been in that category for a while. computers, abstractions, systems — that was my leverage. the gap is narrowing fast, and i don't think the interesting question is whether to panic. it's what to orient toward next.

the frame i've landed on is: go where the data is thin, where the atoms actually matter, where nobody has written the textbook yet. two fields keep bubbling up — biotech and space — and a specific corner of biotech has had my attention: xenobots.

xenobots are tiny clusters of living cells that are not any animal. the cells themselves are ordinary — originally frog cells, more recently human cells too. the shape is designed on a computer by an algorithm that searches for a body plan that can do something useful, and then the cells are either sculpted or left to self-assemble in a dish. what you get is a millimetric blob that swims, coordinates, heals when cut, carries cargo. a living robot, basically, built from skin cells.

the part that broke my intuition: they can reproduce. not genetically — mechanically. a parent shaped like a tiny pac-man pushes loose cells into its mouth, the pile sticks together, and a new one pops out and swims off. no animal on earth does this. the algorithm just found it by searching.

more recently, researchers built the same kind of thing from adult human cells and got a surprise. drop a cluster of these bots on a patch of damaged human neurons, and in about 72 hours the tissue bridges the gap. the bots have zero neural cells in them. nobody really knows how it works.

what all of this points to is a strange idea: the genome isn't really the instruction manual for the body. it's more like a parts list. the final shape is computed by the cells together, and that computation can be re-targeted. a two-headed worm, grown without editing a single gene. today.

which changes what "building with biology" means. it stops being about editing genes and starts being about specifying shapes and behaviors. you give the cells a goal, they figure out how to get there.

and this is where the project idea i can't stop turning over in my head lives.

quick aside on irony: the same thing making my old moat obsolete is what made this idea actually testable. iterating with an AI that pushes back, searches papers, argues with your numbers — that turns "it would be cool if" into something that starts to resemble a plan. i've been bouncing this one around with grok for a while, and what looked like a vague dream is slowly shaping into something you could actually stress-test.

the shape: if you have programmable living matter that is microscopic, cheap, and self-replicating, the most interesting place to point it is space. you don't need heavy industrial infrastructure to seed a planet. you need cells, a target shape, and a launch window. millions or billions of tiny living things, designed on a computer, carried somewhere new.

how it gets there: a probe lands on a patch of a few hectares — small enough to be a realistic first test. the biobots travel pre-assembled and dormant, packed tight in protective medium for the trip. contact with the soil wakes them up — the trigger is physical, residual humidity or a chemical gradient from the regolith. from that point everything happens in parallel.

the plan is four classes of biobots, all released at once:
- detox — breaks down perchlorates in martian regolith (around 0.5–1% of the soil, reduced locally in 5–15 days by the enzymatic pathway bacteria like azospira oryzae already use on earth)
- builder — aggregates particles into stable structure once a patch is neutralized
- seeder — releases pioneer microbes (chroococcidiopsis-type, already known to survive extreme conditions) once the local pH is ok
- stabilizer — forms light-harvesting mats under martian CO2

no communication between them. each class just reacts to its own local trigger (toxin gradient, pH, humidity, light). the global effect emerges from running millions of units per square meter in parallel.

the numbers that ground it: biobots at 30–500 μm, moving at 10–20 μm/s on cold dry regolith, active for 2–6 weeks before degrading into biodegradable debris. a 10 cm² patch becomes partially viable in 1–2 weeks. a full hectare shows viable islands in 4–6 weeks. by 2–3 months the original biobots are mostly gone and the pioneer microbes they seeded are taking over. the biobots themselves are a one-shot catalyst.

progress is patchy by design — islands of viability, not a uniform sheet. you'd need repeated drops to cover a meaningful area, kill-switches to prevent anything running away. none of it reads as impossible — that's the part that keeps pulling me back.

the usual image of space colonization is rockets, habitats, humans in suits. but the most efficient thing you can bring to a dead planet isn't equipment — it's life itself. programmable, microscopic, alive. not machines preparing the ground for life to show up later. life, arriving.

what pulls me to this is the combination:
- the raw material is dna, and humans produce it in embarrassing quantities
- the hard part — cell culture, figuring out what actually makes these things work — sits almost entirely in the unwritten part of human knowledge
- a fine-tuned model can soak up everything humans have written. it can't soak up what we haven't written yet.

where i end up is something like: the most interesting work for the next decade might be the work that teaches the model, not the work the model can already do. and the weirdest, most open-ended corner of that work is the one where tiny living things go somewhere humans can't follow, and start building.

none of this is certain. it might take a century. it might hit a wall i can't see. but when i ask where the frontier still needs wet atoms and original thinking, this is where the arrow points.

title: reading before writing
body:
i'd like to write down my reflections honestly. but whenever i try, most of what comes out feels too basic to be worth reading — probably already thought through centuries ago and written down better, in books we wouldn't even call philosophical by today's standards.

if i actually want to think something that hasn't already been said better, i first need to catch up. at minimum, reach the level of the canonical philosophers — the ones already read by millions before me.

so that's where i'm starting: reading. trying to do it every day, juggling 2–3 books at once. marx and nietzsche first.

balancing this with my day job isn't easy. work eats most of my time. but i'm making room where i can. one book every two months feels like an honest pace — realistic without being lazy.

title: what am i thinking about
body: open thread. half-formed ideas, questions, things i can't stop chewing on.
